questionText: A machine fills bottles with volume $X$ ml, modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Quality records show that $2\%$ of bottles contain less than $495$ ml and $10\%$ contain more than $515$ ml. (a) Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to $3$ significant figures. (b) Bottles are rejected if they contain less than $498$ ml or more than $512$ ml. Find the percentage of bottles rejected.

steps.description: State the model and the two given facts
steps.explanation: The fill volume is normal with unknown $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ‘$2\%$ below $495$’ gives $P(X<495)=0.02$ and ‘$10\%$ above $515$’ gives $P(X>515)=0.10$. These fix the two parameters.
Standardising $495$ with $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ keeps the lower-tail area at $0.02$.
Only $2\%$ of the area lies below this point, so it sits far into the left tail and the standardised value is a large negative number.
Equating the standardised value of $495$ with $z_1$ gives the first equation.

steps.description: Convert and standardise the second condition
steps.explanation: Since $10\%$ exceed $515$, exactly $90\%$ fall below it; converting to the lower tail lets us read $z_2$ from the inverse normal.
Equating the standardised value of $515$ with $z_2$ gives the second simultaneous equation.
Subtracting the two ‘value $-\ \mu = z\sigma$’ forms cancels $\mu$; the $20$ ml gap spans about $3.34$ standard deviations.
Dividing the volume gap by the standardised gap gives a standard deviation of about $6.00$ ml.
From the first equation $\mu = 495 + 2.0537\,\sigma$. The mean fill of about $507$ ml lies above $495$, consistent with only $2\%$ falling that low.
To three significant figures the model is $X \sim N(507,\ 6.00^2)$ ml, answering part (a).

steps.description: Set up part (b): the rejection probability
steps.explanation: A bottle is rejected in either tail, so the rejection probability is the sum of the two separate tail probabilities using the parameters just found.
The lower cut-off $498$ is $1.5$ standard deviations below the mean, giving a lower-tail area of about $0.0668$.
The upper cut-off $512$ is $0.83$ standard deviations above the mean, leaving an upper-tail area of about $0.2023$.
Because the two rejection regions do not overlap, we simply add their probabilities to get the total chance of rejection.
About $26.9\%$ of bottles are rejected. This is high — most rejections come from the upper limit $512$, which sits below the mean, suggesting the machine is set to overfill relative to that limit.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $\mu \approx 507$ ml, $\sigma \approx 6.00$ ml. (b) about $26.9\%$ of bottles are rejected.
